explicit_text: I was living in New Carlisle, Ohio, at my parents' house when I decided to join the military. Growing up there, I was influenced by my family’s military background, especially my father who was a United States Marine. It was a small town near Dayton and Springfield, and coming from that rural area shaped a lot of my perspective before I enlisted.

Growing up there, I was influenced by my family’s military background, especially my father who was a United States Marine. explicit_text: I joined the United States Marine Corps because I wanted to be the best, following in my father's footsteps who was also a Marine. My experience started with boot camp at either Parris Island or San Diego, where the discipline and intensity were unlike anything I'd seen before. Serving as a Marine shaped my life profoundly, especially through my deployments during Operation Iraqi Freedom.

My experience started with boot camp at either the East Coast or West Coast recruit training depots, where the discipline and intensity were unlike anything I'd seen before. Serving in this branch shaped my life profoundly, especially through my deployments during the early 2000s conflicts in the Middle East.

explicit_text: My father was drafted into the United States Marine Corps during the Vietnam War, and his service was a big influence on me joining the military. Although I did not serve in Vietnam myself, the legacy of that war and my father's experience were always present in my decision to enlist. It was a part of my family history that I carried with me into my own service.

entity: Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island
explicit_text: When I arrived at Marine Corps Recruit Depot Parris Island, I vividly remember stepping onto the yellow footprints, which marked where I was to stand during boot camp. The drill instructors immediately began their intense training, and it was a shock to the system but also exhilarating. Parris Island was one of the places where I learned discipline and the fundamentals of being a Marine.
implicit_text: When I arrived at the East Coast recruit training depot famous for its intense boot camp, I vividly remember stepping onto the yellow footprints, which marked where I was to stand during training. That location was one of the places where I learned discipline and the fundamentals of becoming a Marine.

entity: Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego
explicit_text: Marine Corps Recruit Depot San Diego was another location where recruits like me experienced the rigorous start of Marine life. Like Parris Island, it had the iconic yellow footprints that marked the beginning of our transformation. The training there was tough, and the drill instructors were strict, but it prepared me well for my future deployments.

explicit_text: During boot camp, I remember several drill instructors, including Senior Drill Instructor Staff Sergeant Pree and Staff Sergeant Mejia. These Staff Sergeants were key figures in our training, enforcing discipline and pushing us to meet the high standards of the Marine Corps. Their leadership was memorable and shaped much of my early military experience.

explicit_text: I served two tours during Operation Iraqi Freedom, first in 2003 and then later during Operation Iraqi Freedom Four. My first deployment started in Kuwait at Mutalla Ranch and then moved into Iraq, reaching Baghdad. The second tour was at Al Asad Airbase in Al Anbar Province, near Syria, and also included a stint in Haqlaniyah. These deployments were challenging but also gave me a unique perspective on the region and the Marine Corps' role there.
implicit_text: I served two tours during the early 2000s Middle Eastern conflict, first in 2003 and then later during the fourth phase of the operation. My first deployment started in Kuwait at Mutalla Ranch and then moved into Iraq, reaching Baghdad. The second tour was at a major airbase in western Iraq near Syria, and also included a stint in a northern province town. These deployments were challenging but also gave me a unique perspective on the region and the Marine infantry's role there.

explicit_text: My first deployment during Operation Iraqi Freedom began in Kuwait at a place called Mutalla Ranch, which was really just desert. The temperatures there were extreme, reaching 110 to 115 degrees, and it was a harsh environment to adjust to. From Kuwait, we moved into Iraq, crossing the border on the main MSR to Baghdad, marking the start of our combat operations.

explicit_text: Mutalla Ranch in Kuwait was where we were stationed at the start of Operation Iraqi Freedom One. Despite its name, it was really just a desert area with no real ranch features. explicit_text: Iraq was the main area where I served during both of my deployments in Operation Iraqi Freedom. I traveled from the southern border near Kuwait all the way to Baghdad on my first tour, and on my second tour, I was stationed further north at Al Asad Airbase and Haqlaniyah in Al Anbar Province. The country has a harsh climate but also beautiful historical sites like the Tigris and Euphrates rivers, which I found remarkable despite the conflict around us.

explicit_text: During my second tour in Iraq, I was stationed at Al Asad Airbase, located in the northern part of Al Anbar Province. It was a strategic location not far from the Syrian border, and we operated in a challenging environment with seasonal climate changes. Al Asad was one of the main bases where we conducted our missions during Operation Iraqi Freedom Four.

explicit_text: One of the most memorable sights during my deployment in Iraq was seeing the Tigris River. It’s part of the cradle of civilization and a beautiful natural landmark that I never expected to see firsthand. Despite the conflict around us, the Tigris stood out as a symbol of history and life in the region.

explicit_text: I was proud to be part of the mortuary affairs company that was awarded the Presidential Unit Citation during Operation Iraqi Freedom. This was notable because mortuary affairs, or Graves Registration, had not been used since Vietnam until it was reinstated for our mission. Receiving this citation recognized the reverent and important work we did during the conflict.
